option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Flyway Checksum Mismatch Fix
## Date: October 28, 2025
## Problem
``` Error creating bean with name 'flywayInitializer': Validate failed: Migrations have failed validation Migration checksum mismatch for migration version 1 -> Applied to database : 474943937 -> Resolved locally : 738141842 ```
## Root Cause
The Flyway migration file `V1__create_users_table.sql` was modified **after** it had already been applied to the database. Flyway stores a checksum of each migration in the `flyway_schema_history` table. When the file changes, the checksum changes, causing validation to fail.
## Why This Happened
During development, we: 1. Applied V1 migration (original version) 2. Later modified V1 to add more fields/indexes 3. Flyway detected the mismatch and rejected the migration
## Solution Implemented
### 1. **Created FlywayConfig with Auto-Repair (Development)**
**File:** `shared/src/main/kotlin/app/venues/shared/persistence/config/FlywayConfig.kt`
**What it does:** - In **development** environments (profiles: `dev`, `local`, `default`): - Automatically runs `flyway.repair()` to fix checksum mismatches - Then validates and migrates - Allows developers to iterate on migrations during early development
- In **production** environments (profiles: `prod`, `production`): - NO auto-repair - Strict validation - Fails immediately on checksum mismatch - Forces manual intervention
### 2. **Updated application.properties**
**File:** `app/src/main/resources/application.properties`
Added: ```properties # Clean database on startup (DEVELOPMENT ONLY) spring.flyway.clean-disabled=false # Repair schema history table (handled by FlywayConfig) spring.flyway.repair-on-migrate=false ```
## How It Works
### Development Flow: ``` Application Start ↓ FlywayConfig detects 'dev' profile ↓ Runs flyway.repair() ↓ Fixes checksum in flyway_schema_history table ↓ Validates migrations ↓ Applies pending migrations ↓ Application starts successfully ✅ ```
### Production Flow: ``` Application Start ↓ FlywayConfig detects 'prod' profile ↓ Validates migrations (NO repair) ↓ If checksum mismatch → FAIL ❌ ↓ Requires manual investigation and repair ```
## Best Practices Going Forward
### ✅ DO: 1. **Never modify migrations after they're applied to production** 2. **Create new migrations** for schema changes (V3, V4, etc.) 3. **Use repair in development only** during initial development 4. **Test migrations in staging** before production 5. **Keep migration files immutable** once deployed
### ❌ DON'T: 1. **Don't modify deployed migrations** in production 2. **Don't use auto-repair** in production 3. **Don't skip validation** in production 4. **Don't clean database** in production (clean-disabled=true)
## Alternative Solutions
If you prefer NOT to use auto-repair, here are alternatives:
### Option 1: Manual Flyway Repair (Command Line) ```bash # Using Gradle ./gradlew flywayRepair
# Using Maven mvn flyway:repair ```
### Option 2: Drop and Recreate Database (Development Only) ```sql -- Connect to PostgreSQL DROP DATABASE venues; CREATE DATABASE venues; ```
### Option 3: Update Checksum Manually (Not Recommended) ```sql -- Update the checksum in flyway_schema_history UPDATE flyway_schema_history SET checksum = 738141842 WHERE version = '1'; ```
### Option 4: Delete Schema History Entry (Development Only) ```sql -- Delete the problematic entry DELETE FROM flyway_schema_history WHERE version = '1'; -- Flyway will reapply it ```
## Migration Versioning Strategy
Going forward, follow this pattern:
``` V1__create_users_table.sql ← Initial schema (NEVER MODIFY) V2__add_user_enhancements.sql ← Add new fields V3__create_booking_tables.sql ← New features V4__add_user_referral_indexes.sql ← Performance improvements ... ```
Each migration is **immutable** and **cumulative**.
## Testing
After applying this fix:
1. **Stop the application** 2. **Start the application** 3. **Check logs** for: ``` Running Flyway repair to fix checksum mismatches... Flyway repair completed successfully Migration validation successful Applied X migration(s) ``` 4. **Verify database** has all tables and data
## Production Deployment Checklist
Before deploying to production:
- [ ] Change profile to `prod` or `production` - [ ] Verify `spring.flyway.clean-disabled=true` - [ ] Test migrations on staging environment - [ ] Create database backup - [ ] Review all migration files - [ ] Ensure no migrations were modified - [ ] Document rollback plan
## Summary
✅ **Fixed:** Flyway checksum mismatch error ✅ **Added:** Auto-repair for development environments ✅ **Protected:** Production from auto-repair ✅ **Documented:** Best practices for migrations
The application should now start successfully in development mode, automatically repairing any checksum mismatches.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) Flow Explanation
## Complete Flow: How Only Admins Can Access `/api/v1/users/{id}`
### Step-by-Step Breakdown
``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 1: User Logs In │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
POST /api/v1/auth/user/login { "email": "admin@venues.gov", "password": "Admin@123" }
↓
UserAuthService.login() checks credentials ↓ User found in DB with role = ADMIN ↓ JWT token generated: { "sub": "admin@venues.gov", "userId": 1, "role": "ADMIN", ← Role embedded in token! "iat": 1698508800, "exp": 1698595200 }
↓
Response: { "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...", "tokenType": "Bearer", "expiresIn": 86400000, "user": { ... } }
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 2: User Makes Request with JWT Token │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
GET /api/v1/users/123 Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 3: JwtAuthenticationFilter Intercepts Request │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
JwtAuthenticationFilter.doFilterInternal() │ ├─ Extract token from "Authorization: Bearer <token>" │ ├─ Call jwtService.getUserIdFromToken(jwt) │ → Returns: 1 │ ├─ Call jwtService.getUsernameFromToken(jwt) │ → Returns: "admin@venues.gov" │ ├─ Call jwtService.getRoleFromToken(jwt) │ → Returns: "ADMIN" ← Role extracted from JWT! │ ├─ Check if token expired │ → No, still valid │ ├─ Create Spring Security Authority: │ authorities = [SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_ADMIN")] │ ^^^^^ Note: Spring adds "ROLE_" prefix │ ├─ Create Authentication Principal (user info): │ principal = { │ "userId": 1, │ "email": "admin@venues.gov", │ "role": "ADMIN" │ } │ ├─ Create UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken: │ authentication = UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken( │ principal, │ null, │ authorities = ["ROLE_ADMIN"] ← This is what Spring Security checks! │ ) │ └─ Store in SecurityContext: SecurityContextHolder.getContext().authentication = authentication
↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 4: Request Continues Through Spring Security Filter Chain │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Spring Security's AuthorizationFilter checks: │ ├─ Does user have authentication? → YES ├─ What authorities does user have? → ["ROLE_ADMIN"] └─ Request continues...
↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 5: Spring MVC Routes Request to Controller │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
DispatcherServlet finds matching handler: @GetMapping("/{id}") in UserController ↓ Method: getUserById(id: Long)
↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 6: @PreAuthorize Annotation Checks Role │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
BEFORE executing getUserById(), Spring Security checks:
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ This SpEL expression is evaluated!
Spring Security checks: │ ├─ Get current Authentication from SecurityContext │ → authentication.authorities = ["ROLE_ADMIN"] │ ├─ Evaluate: hasRole('ADMIN') │ → Spring Security converts 'ADMIN' to 'ROLE_ADMIN' │ → Checks if 'ROLE_ADMIN' exists in authorities │ → YES! ["ROLE_ADMIN"] contains "ROLE_ADMIN" │ └─ Authorization: GRANTED ✅
↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Step 7: Method Executes │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
fun getUserById(id: Long) { val user = userService.getUserById(id) return ResponseEntity.ok(ApiResponse.success(UserMapper.toResponse(user))) }
↓
Response: 200 OK { "success": true, "data": { "id": 123, "email": "user@test.com", "firstName": "Test", ... } } ```
---
## What Happens if a Regular USER Tries to Access?
``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ USER with role=USER tries to access /api/v1/users/123 │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
JWT Token contains: { "role": "USER" ← Regular user, not admin }
↓
JwtAuthenticationFilter processes: authorities = [SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_USER")] ^^^^^^^^^^ Only USER role!
↓
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") checks: │ ├─ Get authorities: ["ROLE_USER"] ├─ Evaluate: hasRole('ADMIN') │ → Checks if 'ROLE_ADMIN' exists in ["ROLE_USER"] │ → NO! Not found! │ └─ Authorization: DENIED ❌
↓
Spring Security's AccessDeniedHandler is invoked:
response.sendError(403, "Forbidden: Access Denied")
↓
Response: 403 Forbidden { "success": false, "error": { "code": "AUTHORIZATION_FAILED", "message": "You do not have permission to access this resource" } }
❌ Method getUserById() is NEVER executed! ```
---
## Key Components
### 1. **@EnableMethodSecurity** (in SecurityConfig)
```kotlin @Configuration @EnableWebSecurity @EnableMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true, securedEnabled = true, jsr250Enabled = true) class SecurityConfig { ... } ```
This enables method-level security annotations like: - `@PreAuthorize` - Check before method execution - `@PostAuthorize` - Check after method execution - `@Secured` - Simple role check - `@RolesAllowed` - JSR-250 annotation
### 2. **@PreAuthorize SpEL Expressions**
Spring Expression Language (SpEL) expressions you can use:
```kotlin // Single role check @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
// Multiple roles (OR) @PreAuthorize("hasAnyRole('ADMIN', 'MODERATOR')")
// Multiple roles (AND) @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') and hasRole('SUPER_USER')")
// Check authority (without ROLE_ prefix) @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('WRITE_PRIVILEGE')")
// Complex expressions @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') or #userId == authentication.principal.userId")
// Method parameter access @PreAuthorize("#userId == authentication.principal.userId or hasRole('ADMIN')") fun updateUser(@PathVariable userId: Long) { ... } ```
### 3. **Spring Security Authority Hierarchy**
``` Authentication ├─ Principal (user info) ├─ Credentials (password - usually null after authentication) └─ Authorities (roles/permissions) └─ List<GrantedAuthority> ├─ SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_USER") ├─ SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_ADMIN") └─ ... ```
### 4. **ROLE_ Prefix Convention**
| What You Write | What Spring Stores | What Gets Checked | |----------------|-------------------|-------------------| | `role = "ADMIN"` in DB | N/A | N/A | | JWT token: `"role": "ADMIN"` | N/A | N/A | | `SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_ADMIN")` | "ROLE_ADMIN" | "ROLE_ADMIN" | | `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` | Checks for "ROLE_ADMIN" | Automatically adds prefix | | `@PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('ROLE_ADMIN')")` | Checks for "ROLE_ADMIN" | Uses exact string |
---
## Security Flow Diagram
``` ┌──────────────┐ │ Browser │ └──────┬───────┘ │ GET /api/v1/users/123 │ Authorization: Bearer <JWT> ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ JwtAuthenticationFil │ ← Extracts JWT, creates Authentication │ ter │ with authorities: ["ROLE_ADMIN"] └──────┬───────────────┘ │ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ AuthorizationFilter │ ← Checks if user is authenticated └──────┬───────────────┘ │ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ DispatcherServlet │ ← Routes to controller └──────┬───────────────┘ │ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ MethodSecurityInter │ ← @PreAuthorize check happens HERE! │ ceptor │ Evaluates: hasRole('ADMIN') └──────┬───────────────┘ Checks: ["ROLE_ADMIN"] contains "ROLE_ADMIN"? │ Result: ✅ YES → Continue │ ❌ NO → Throw AccessDeniedException ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ UserController │ ← Only executed if authorization passed │ .getUserById() │ └──────┬───────────────┘ │ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ Response │ └──────────────────────┘ ```
---
## Testing
### Test as ADMIN (Should work ✅):
```bash # 1. Login as admin curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/auth/user/login \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "email": "admin@venues.gov", "password": "Admin@123" }'
# Response contains token: # { "token": "eyJhbG...", ... }
# 2. Use token to access admin endpoint curl -X GET http://localhost:8080/api/v1/users/1 \ -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG..."
# Response: 200 OK ✅ # { "success": true, "data": { "id": 1, ... } } ```
### Test as USER (Should fail ❌):
```bash # 1. Login as regular user curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v1/auth/user/login \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "email": "user@test.com", "password": "User@123" }'
# 2. Try to access admin endpoint curl -X GET http://localhost:8080/api/v1/users/1 \ -H "Authorization: Bearer <user_token>"
# Response: 403 Forbidden ❌ # { "success": false, "error": { "code": "AUTHORIZATION_FAILED", ... } } ```
---
## Summary
**How roles work:** 1. ✅ Role stored in database (USER or ADMIN) 2. ✅ Role embedded in JWT token when user logs in 3. ✅ JwtAuthenticationFilter extracts role and creates authority "ROLE_ADMIN" 4. ✅ Authority stored in SecurityContext 5. ✅ `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` checks if authority exists 6. ✅ If yes → method executes 7. ✅ If no → 403 Forbidden
**Why only admins can access `/users/{id}`:** - The `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")` annotation on `getUserById()` method enforces that only users with "ROLE_ADMIN" authority can execute the method - Regular users have "ROLE_USER" authority, which doesn't match, so they get 403 Forbidden - This happens **before** the method executes, so unauthorized users never reach the business logic

option.name: originalContent
updatedContent
originalContent
updatedContent
